callout.executive-summary:
This report tests the hypothesis that volatility is rough — that the time variation of volatility itself follows an irregular path that trembles at fine scales. Path smoothness is measured by the Hurst exponent $H$ ($0

The central quantitative result is the option skew. For maturity $T$, plot implied volatility (the volatility backed out of option prices) against strike; the slope of that curve at the money (strike equal to the forward price) is the ATM skew $b(T)$. Rough-model theory predicts the short-maturity power law $|b(T)|\propto T^{{H-1/2}}$. Simulating the rough Bergomi model implemented here (a stochastic-volatility model whose forward variance is driven by fBM-type noise, $H={h:g}$) gives a fitted exponent $\beta={beta:.3f}$ against the reference $H-1/2={h_minus_half:.2f}$. The implementation passes two analytic checks — expected variance stays on the configured forward-variance curve, and the discounted spot is a martingale — and the Heston benchmark comparison uses common random numbers (identical draws fed to both models, so differences reflect dynamics rather than luck).

The microstructure and measurement results are diagnostics, not identification. Generating order flow with Hawkes processes (self-exciting point processes in which past trades trigger future ones) shows that near-critical self-excitation alone creates clustered activity and a rough-looking realized-variance proxy (a volatility stand-in built from rolling squared returns). Varying observation noise and sampling, however, moves the estimated $\hat H$ materially — so observing $\hat H<1/2$ does not by itself identify a fractional data-generating process.
callout.conceptual-map:
The reading path is roughness → volatility dynamics → option skew → microstructure diagnostics. Each arrow is an experiment, not a claim that one mechanism uniquely causes the next.

Rough paths→Rough log-volatility→rBergomi prices→ATM skew↔Hawkes proxy
callout.mathematical-definitions: $H$ controls local increment scaling. The covariance, structure function, rBergomi variance normalization, skew power law, and Hawkes intensity below define the quantities used throughout the report. Every symbol appearing in these equations is defined in the variable table below the gallery.
callout.configuration: All evidence is locally generated under one resolved profile. Counts are Monte Carlo paths or event-process observations; option uncertainty is reported by standard error and delta-method IV error.
callout.fbm-path-comparison: $H$ changes path regularity, not simply amplitude. Use the selector to compare paths generated on the same horizon; lower $H$ creates denser fine-scale oscillations.
callout.local-zoom: The regularity difference survives magnification. A short window separates local texture from broad path range, preventing “rough” from being confused with “large variance.”
callout.fgn-increments: Rough increments alternate more sharply. The visual comparison is descriptive; the ACF and scaling regression supply the quantitative evidence.
callout.increment-acf: Levels can look persistent while increments are anti-persistent. For $H<1/2$ the first-lag increment correlation is negative, whereas $H>1/2$ produces positive dependence.
callout.structure-functions: The fitted second-order slope follows $2H$. Only a bounded log-spaced lag range is used, limiting the high-variance long-lag tail.
callout.hurst-recovery: Finite-sample uncertainty is material even on clean fBM. Longer paths tighten the distribution, but regression standard errors do not capture every source of estimator dispersion.
callout.estimator-bias: Bias depends on $H$, estimator, and sample length. This baseline is necessary before interpreting noisier volatility proxies.
callout.ou-versus-fou: Matched broad dispersion does not imply matched local regularity. The fractional OU approximation produces sharper small-scale movement; it is a visualization-grade Euler construction, not an exact stationary sampler.
callout.rough-bergomi-paths: The normalized Volterra driver produces abrupt variance movement while preserving $\mathbb{E}[V_t]=\xi_0(t)$. The spot process uses the left-point variance and an exact discrete log-Euler martingale step.
callout.heston-comparison: Common spot-driver normals isolate dynamics rather than shock luck. Heston is Markovian and full-truncated; the parameter match is broad rather than a calibration equivalence.
callout.terminal-distributions: Different variance dynamics change pathwise and terminal dispersion. Histograms are density-normalized and use the same Monte Carlo path count.
callout.iv-smiles: Short-maturity smiles are the noisiest and most model-sensitive. Use the maturity selector and read the error bars before interpreting wing differences.
callout.iv-surface: The rough surface concentrates curvature and skew at short maturities. The heatmap uses identical $k$–$T$ coordinates across models.
callout.atm-skew-term: Smaller $H$ produces larger short-maturity skew magnitude. Each point is a weighted local-quadratic derivative with at least five valid IV observations.
callout.skew-scaling: The fitted direction agrees with $T^{H-1/2}$. Agreement is finite-maturity and simulation-specific; the report does not treat it as exact asymptotic confirmation.
callout.hawkes-events: Self-excitation turns rate-matched events into bursts. The Poisson, stable, and near-critical scenarios share the same target unconditional rate but differ in branching ratio.
callout.order-flow-price: Signed imbalance translates bursts into a clustered pedagogical price. This construction is intentionally simple and is not a structural market-impact model.
callout.volatility-proxy: Conditional-intensity bursts co-locate with elevated rolling RV proxies. The reported effective $H$ is explicitly an empirical diagnostic only.
callout.noise-bias: Measurement choices can manufacture or suppress apparent roughness. Use the preprocessing selector: raw, aggregated, and pre-averaged observations produce different $H$ biases from the same latent paths.
callout.establishes:
• Davies–Harte fBM reproduces clean scaling and estimator-recovery patterns.
  • The exact joint-Gaussian rBergomi grid preserves the configured forward variance within Monte Carlo error.
  • Short-maturity skew steepens as $H$ decreases in these finite simulations.
  • Near-critical Hawkes flow is sufficient to create clustered activity and a rough-looking proxy.
callout.does-not-establish:
• An estimated $\hat H<1/2$ does not identify a fractional data-generating process.
  • The Hawkes proxy is not a derivation of rough Heston or a market calibration.
  • Finite-maturity skew fits do not prove the asymptotic exponent exactly.
  • Broadly matched Heston and rBergomi parameters are not economically equivalent calibrations.
callout.limitations-next-steps:
The next useful tests are robustness tests, not more decorative complexity.

  1. Replace synthetic $\xi_0$ with a calibrated forward-variance curve and propagate quote uncertainty.
  2. Add hybrid/FFT Volterra schemes and benchmark convergence against the exact-grid operator.
  3. Use variance-reduction methods for short-wing IV and skew estimates.
  4. Test noise-robust roughness estimators and alternative sampling schemes.
  5. Fit Hawkes kernels from event data only after addressing seasonality and market-state conditioning.

Further questions

How stable are the conclusions under alternative leverage, vol-of-vol, and maturity grids? Which estimator remains least biased under realistic price discreteness and asynchronous sampling?

section.prior-literature: Prior literature: problems, proposals, results, and open questions
variable.fbm: Fractional Brownian motion at times $t$ and $s$: a centered Gaussian process with $B_0^H=0$ and variance $t^{2H}$.
variable.hurst: Hurst exponent in $(0,1)$. Controls local regularity: $H<1/2$ is rough and $H=1/2$ recovers standard Brownian motion. The lab's reference value is $H=0.1$.
variable.structure: Structure function of order $q$: the $q$-th absolute moment of increments at lag $\Delta$; the estimators fit its log–log slope $qH$.
variable.process: The process under study — here an fBM path or log-volatility.
variable.lag: Increment lag; fits use a bounded log-spaced lag grid only.
variable.order: Moment order; for a monofractal process the scaling exponent is linear in $q$ (equal to $qH$).
variable.variance: Instantaneous variance at time $t$; volatility is $\sigma_t=\sqrt{V_t}$.
variable.forward_variance: Initial forward-variance curve; the exponential correction enforces $\mathbb{E}[V_t]=\xi_0(t)$ exactly at the simulated grid points.
variable.vol_of_vol: Volatility of volatility; scales the fluctuations of the variance path.
variable.volterra: Volterra (Riemann–Liouville) fBM, $\widetilde W_t^H=\sqrt{2H}\int_0^t (t-s)^{H-1/2}\,dW_s$; normalized so that $\mathrm{Var}(\widetilde W_t^H)=t^{2H}$.
variable.implied_vol: Black–Scholes implied volatility; the slope $\partial\sigma_{\mathrm{imp}}/\partial k$ at $k=0$ is the ATM skew $b(T)$.
variable.log_moneyness: Forward log-moneyness $k=\log(K/F_T)$, with strike $K$ and forward price $F_T$.
variable.maturity: Option maturity in years; the $T^{H-1/2}$ power law is a finite-maturity diagnostic of the $T\to 0$ asymptotic.
variable.intensity: Conditional intensity of side $i$ (buy/sell): expected events per unit time given the history.
variable.kernel: Excitation kernel: the impact of past side-$j$ events on side-$i$ intensity. The spectral radius of the kernel-integral matrix is the branching ratio; values near 1 are near-critical.
variable.counting: Counting process of side $j$ (cumulative events up to time $t$); $dN_s^j$ denotes individual events.
